scope_note: R4 pre-implementation: tests compile against todo!() stubs. Review is of test-file quality, assertion strength, clarity, TDD-contract fidelity, and named-compromise regression fidelity. Coverage gaps belong to rust-test-coverage; integration semantics belong to qa-expert.

findings.claim: Meta-test `scaffolder_smoke_test_asserts_all_six_exit_criteria` uses broken regex. Pattern `/\\bit\\s*\\(/g` contains double-escaped backslashes, so the regex matches literal `\bit\s*\(` characters in the template file — not `it(` calls. The match count will always be 0, so the assertion `expect(itCount).toBe(6)` will always fail, but more importantly: if the double-escape is silently corrected later without updating the expectation, the test will pass vacuously (match count = some number that happens to be 6). This is the R4 TDD-fidelity catch — the regex as-written can never succeed against real source.
findings.evidence: Line 50: `const itCount = (template.match(/\\bit\\s*\\(/g) ?? []).length;`. In a JS regex literal, `\b` is the word-boundary escape; `\\b` encodes a literal backslash followed by `b`. Template files do not contain literal backslashes before `it(`, so match returns null → 0. The intended pattern is `/\bit\s*\(/g`.
findings.fix: Replace with `/\bit\s*\(/g` (single-escape). Verify against the generated template contents before R5 advances.

findings.claim: The TOCTOU named-compromise regression (#1) asserts `completed >= 149 && completed < 250` but the R1 triage + `crates/benten-caps/tests/toctou_iteration.rs` both pin the scope at `DEFAULT_BATCH_BOUNDARY=100` with writes staying visible until the NEXT boundary after the revocation. For revocation at iter 150, the named-compromise window lets writes 101..=200 land; denials begin at 201+. This test's lower bound (`>= 149`) permits an implementation that denies at exactly iter 150 (i.e. per-primitive, not per-batch) — the opposite of what named compromise #1 documents. Test and unit-level `writes_in_current_batch_are_not_retroactively_denied` disagree on the floor.
findings.evidence: Integration file asserts `completed >= 149`; `crates/benten-caps/tests/toctou_iteration.rs::writes_in_current_batch_are_not_retroactively_denied` asserts `successful_write_count() >= 200`. Both claim to pin named compromise #1. If R5 ships a per-primitive check (silently closing the window), the integration test passes and the compromise is violated without any test failing.
findings.fix: Tighten integration assertion to `completed >= 200 && completed < 250` so a per-primitive (early-tightening) implementation fails the test, matching the unit-level contract. Add a comment cross-referencing `r1-triage.md §named-compromises #1` and the `DEFAULT_BATCH_BOUNDARY` constant.

findings.claim: Three of the five View files assert only `matches!(r, ViewResult::Cids(_))` / `matches!(..., ViewResult::Current(_))` without checking the contents. A View implementation that unconditionally returns `Ok(ViewResult::Cids(vec![]))` (or `Current(None)`) for every query passes every test here. The tests name real behaviors (`view1_read_returns_cids_result`, `view5_read_returns_current_result`) but assert only the variant. An R5 implementer could land a no-op `read` method and all four views' positive tests pass.
findings.evidence: view1 line 40-41: `let r = v.read(&q).unwrap(); assert!(matches!(r, ViewResult::Cids(_)));`. No grant was pre-registered, no update was pushed, and no CID content is checked. view2 and view5 follow the same pattern. Contrast with view3_content_listing.rs which asserts `cids.len() == 3` and pagination counts.
findings.fix: For each view, push at least one `update(&event)` covering the view's input pattern and assert the read result contains the specific CID(s) expected. Minimum: one positive-after-update test per view asserting exact contents, not just variant shape.

findings.claim: Test name advertises MVCC snapshot-isolation semantics ("reader sees point-in-time state") but the body never opens a snapshot, never performs concurrent commits, and writes two distinct nodes under two distinct CIDs — then reads them back. This proves only that `put_node(a); put_node(b); get_node(a) == a; get_node(b) == b`. Any in-memory HashMap satisfies this test. It does NOT test MVCC at all. The test will pass on a backend with zero isolation semantics, yet the name commits the project to MVCC in R4b review.
findings.evidence: Line 36-38: writes `v2` with distinct labels/properties (so distinct CID); line 41 asserts the original CID still resolves to the original node. Distinct CID → distinct key → no overlap → no isolation concern exercised. Real MVCC test requires: open a read-tx, commit a write in another thread/tx, verify the read-tx still sees pre-commit state.
findings.fix: Either rename to `different_keys_read_independently` (honest) or replace the body: snapshot-reader-opens, writer-commits-overwrite-or-delete-on-same-cid, snapshot-reader-still-sees-pre-commit. If the KVBackend shape doesn't expose snapshot handles yet, file a coverage finding instead of silently passing.

findings.claim: The lag assertion `observed + 1 >= expected` is mathematically equivalent to `observed >= expected - 1`, meaning an implementation that always returns `observed = expected - 1` (i.e. a constant 1-write lag) passes every iteration. Worse, `observed >= 0 >= expected - 1` when `expected == 1` is trivially true. The test never checks `observed <= expected` (upper bound), so an implementation returning every stored node plus phantom ghosts also passes.
findings.evidence: Line 73-77: `assert!(observed + 1 >= expected, ...)`. First iteration: expected=1, observed could be 0 → 0+1>=1 ✓. Last iteration: expected=10, observed could be 9 → 9+1>=10 ✓. Nothing checks observed <= expected.
findings.fix: Change to `assert!(observed <= expected && observed + 1 >= expected, ...)` or, stronger, assert `expected - 1 <= observed <= expected` with a comment naming the 1-write-lag bound. Matches the named contract "lag never exceeds 1 write" (two-sided).

findings.claim: Property-based test has case range `0usize..5` (only 5 cases) and asserts only `std::mem::discriminant(&r_inc) == std::mem::discriminant(&r_reb)` — i.e. both results are `ViewResult::Cids(_)`. Two views that both return empty vecs pass this. Two views that disagree on every CID pass this. The property-name advertises incremental-equals-rebuild equivalence; the body asserts only shape equivalence.
findings.evidence: Line 100-119: `let mut rebuilt = ContentListingView::new("Post"); for _ in 0..n { incremental.update(&post_created()).unwrap(); } ... rebuilt.rebuild().unwrap(); ... prop_assert!(discriminant(&r_inc) == discriminant(&r_reb));`. The `rebuild()` never sees the same events the incremental path sees (they come from a backend in the real case), and the final assertion compares discriminants only.
findings.fix: Either (a) assert exact equality on the inner `Vec<Cid>` including length and CID values, and feed the rebuild path from the same source-of-truth the incremental path is consuming; or (b) add a `// PHASE 1 STUB: tightens to full equality in R5` comment and file a coverage note — but keep the relaxed discriminant check out of the prop-test if it can't distinguish a no-op from a correct implementation.

findings.claim: Test body is a compile-time-only witness: it declares `fn _typecheck_iterate_max_is_u64()` inside the test and never calls it, never asserts anything at runtime. At TDD-contract level this is effectively `#[test] fn name() {}` — an empty body. If `iterate`'s signature quietly accepts `i64`, the inner function still compiles (i64 -> u64 via literal inference for `0u64`) and the test passes without exercising the claim. The test name pins a runtime/DSL boundary behavior it cannot check from Rust.
findings.evidence: Lines 91-107: The entire test body is a nested fn definition + a comment. No assertions, no runtime check. If an R5 refactor changes `iterate(..., max: i64)`, the `0u64` literal still compiles (implicit conversion) and the test silently passes while the contract is broken.
findings.fix: Either delete this test (it's load-bearing only at the napi/TS boundary, which has its own B8 test) or split into a real compile-fail test using `trybuild` / a dedicated `compile_fail` doctest that breaks if `iterate`'s signature widens. Alternatively, assert at runtime that attempting to construct a builder with `i64::MIN as u64` (wraps) still lands in u64 semantic space.

findings.claim: Marker test's body is `assert_eq!(ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead.as_str(), ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead.as_str())` — a trivially-true self-comparison. The test name promises "documented compromise" semantics but carries zero assertion on the compromise scope. The comment says "This is a pure marker test — no assertion body needed beyond confirming the code is reachable." Per R4 contract, named-compromise tests MUST assert the compromise as documented; this one does not. If `ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead` is renamed or removed in R5, this test still passes until compile-fail surfaces in another file.
findings.evidence: Lines 130-133 contain only `assert_eq!(ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead.as_str(), ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead.as_str())`. Identical operands make this `assert_eq!(x, x)` — tautology.
findings.fix: Replace body with a meaningful compromise assertion: e.g. `assert_eq!(ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead.as_str(), "E_CAP_DENIED_READ")` AND verify the code exists in `docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md` (stringly-compared as in `compromise_6_blake3_collision_resistance_note_in_security_posture`).

findings.claim: Two separate test files cover the same Value::Float NaN/Infinity/±0 rejection contract with overlapping test names (`float_nan_rejected`, `float_infinity_rejected`, `float_zero_and_negative_zero_are_equal_cid` / `float_zero_positive_and_negative_have_same_cid`). R3's unit writer (value_float.rs) and edge-cases writer (float_nan_inf.rs) produced parallel implementations — when R5 lands the code, both suites run and duplicate-test executions double CI time. Worse, the two disagree slightly: value_float.rs uses a single `f64::NAN`; float_nan_inf.rs fuzzes three NaN bit patterns (quiet, signalling, non-default payload). If the implementation canonicalizes quiet NaNs but rejects payload NaNs, behavior diverges and one file passes while the other fails — creating ambiguous CI.
findings.evidence: value_float.rs test `float_nan_rejected` tests `f64::NAN`; float_nan_inf.rs test `float_nan_rejected` iterates `[0x7ff8_0000_0000_0000, 0x7ff8_0000_0000_0001, 0xfff8_0000_0000_0000]`. Same test names, same crate, different test binaries.
findings.fix: Consolidate into one file. Keep the float_nan_inf.rs (stronger — exercises three bit patterns). Remove duplicates from value_float.rs or rename for disambiguation (e.g. `float_nan_any_bit_pattern_rejected`). Rule of thumb: one named contract per test.

findings.claim: Two TS tests use DoS-shaped or sleep-based patterns. (a) index.test.ts allocates `new Uint8Array(32 * 1024 * 1024)` (32MB) in-process to verify size-rejection; if the binding ACCEPTS this payload (e.g. during pre-B8 stub runs), the test process itself allocates 32MB + whatever the napi copy adds — not just a DoS but a flake risk on CI runners with tight memory ceilings. (b) crud.test.ts uses `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1))` to assert `second.createdAt > first.createdAt`. A 1ms sleep is not a reliable monotonicity source; HLC stamping is deterministic under the current spec, so the sleep is either spurious (HLC advances regardless) or load-bearing on wall-clock (flaky on busy CI).
findings.evidence: index.test.ts line 72: `const big = new Uint8Array(32 * 1024 * 1024);`. crud.test.ts line 48: `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1));`.
findings.fix: (a) Use 16MB + 1 byte (the spec threshold) instead of 32MB; or pass a mock Buffer-like object that lies about length. (b) Remove the sleep; assert `second.createdAt >= first.createdAt` (weak monotonicity on identical HLC clocks) or assert deterministic HLC ordering via a seeded clock exposed through a testing API.

findings.claim: The `view*_update_with_*_event_ok` / `view*_rebuild_from_scratch_ok` tests assert only `.unwrap()` succeeds — no side-effect, no state inspection. An update that silently drops events passes. A rebuild that no-ops passes. Combined with finding #3 (vacuous `matches!` on reads), the three views have ZERO tests that prove state mutation actually happens through the trait.
findings.evidence: view1 line 29-30: `let mut v = CapabilityGrantsView::new(); v.update(&grant_event()).unwrap();` — no subsequent read, no state assertion. Same shape in view2 and view5 update tests.
findings.fix: After each update, read back via the same query shape and assert the event's CID now appears in the result set. At minimum: `update(e); let r = view.read(&q_for(e.cid)); assert!(r_cids.contains(&e.cid))`.

findings.claim: The benchmark file is an R3 stub that falls through `open_group_commit` and `open_async` to the default `RedbBackend::open`, measuring the wrong metric under both modes. The comment explicitly acknowledges "the bench still exercises the code path but measures the wrong number." However there is NO mechanism that will break/fail the bench when G2 lands but the benchmarks are not rewired — the TODO comment is the only signal. When G2 actually ships DurabilityMode, both benches will still compile, both will still produce numbers, and CI may pass while the §14.6-derived targets have not actually been exercised.
findings.evidence: Lines 46-54: both `open_group_commit` and `open_async` delegate to `RedbBackend::open(path)`. Only a `TODO(G2)` comment differentiates intent.
findings.fix: Add a compile-time `#[cfg(not(feature = "r3_stub"))]` guard, or a build.rs assertion, or make the functions return `unimplemented!()`. Goal: when G2 lands, removing the stub is a forced edit, not a TODO-chase.

findings.claim: Three files use three different construction idioms for `NoAuthBackend` and three different `WriteContext` field sets, revealing a TDD-contract drift. noauth.rs uses `NoAuthBackend` as a unit struct + `WriteContext { label, scope, actor_cid }`; noauth_proptest.rs uses `NoAuthBackend::new()` + `WriteContext { target_label, is_privileged, actor_hint }`; production_refuses_noauth.rs uses `NoAuthBackend::new()`. These files cannot all compile against a single R5 API — one or two will need to be patched during R5, silently weakening coverage of whichever shape is dropped.
findings.evidence: noauth.rs:15: `let policy = NoAuthBackend;` (unit-struct); noauth_proptest.rs:53: `NoAuthBackend::new()`; noauth.rs:24: `WriteContext { label: "Post", scope: "post:write", actor_cid: None, ..Default::default() }`; noauth_proptest.rs:38: `ctx.target_label = target_label; ctx.is_privileged = is_priv; ctx.actor_hint = actor;`; lib.rs:135: `pub struct NoAuthBackend;` (unit).
findings.fix: Escalate: R3 writers landed inconsistent API expectations. Pick one shape (most likely `WriteContext { target_label, is_privileged, actor_hint }` since that matches the system_zone tests' `is_privileged` field) and rewrite the noauth.rs tests against it. Add a cross-file consistency check to the R3 consolidator going forward.

findings.claim: Compromise #4's regression test scans the CI workflow file for the literal strings `napi-wasm32-compile-check` or `wasm32-unknown-unknown`, and rejects presence of `napi-wasm-runtime`. This is a textual scan of a config artifact — R5 can rename any of these strings during CI refactors and the test fires spuriously OR passes vacuously depending on spelling. Named compromise #4 should assert a semantic property (the WASM runtime check is compile-only) not a string-grep of workflow YAML. Additionally, compromise #6 uses the same string-grep approach (`posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128")`) — a doc with `BLAKE3-256` and the number `128` anywhere (e.g. "128-bit collision resistance" vs "128 users") both pass.
findings.evidence: Lines 134-143: `ci.contains("napi-wasm32-compile-check") || ci.contains("wasm32-unknown-unknown")` + `!ci.contains("napi-wasm-runtime")`. Line 181-186: `posture.contains("BLAKE3") && posture.contains("128")`.
findings.fix: For #4: parse the CI YAML with a real parser (serde_yaml is already a workspace transitive dep), look for a step that runs `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p benten-napi`. For #6: grep for a more specific phrase like `"128-bit collision resistance"` or anchor to a named section (e.g. `## BLAKE3 128-bit posture`).

findings.claim: Test asserts only `ev.stack.len() <= before` — an empty-body RESPOND that leaves the stack unchanged passes, a RESPOND that grows the stack fails. But the name says "pops", which implies `ev.stack.len() < before` (or ==0). A correct implementation that follows the documented semantics (RESPOND is terminal and should clear the frame stack) should cause the assertion to be strict-less-than.
findings.evidence: Line 53-55: `assert!(ev.stack.len() <= before, "RESPOND must not grow the stack beyond its pre-state")`. Test name: `evaluator_pops_on_respond_terminal`. Documented behavior per §2.5 E2: RESPOND terminates.
findings.fix: Change to `assert_eq!(ev.stack.len(), 0)` (RESPOND terminates → stack empty) OR rename test to `evaluator_does_not_grow_stack_on_respond` to match the relaxed assertion. Consistency between name and body is the R4 clarity criterion.

findings.claim: Test name `phase_1_executable_subset_is_eight_primitives` asserts via `executable.len() == 8` + a series of `.contains(&PrimitiveKind::X)` calls for 8 variants — but the 8-element check alone is necessary and the contains-checks for each of the 8 are redundant (any one missing would fail length). More importantly, the test doesn't assert that the phase-2 four (Wait/Stream/Subscribe/Sandbox) are NOT in the list — that contract is in the separate `phase_2_primitives_are_not_phase_1_executable` test. Both together are correct; each alone is weak. Consider merging for clarity.
findings.evidence: Lines 54-67: filter + len check + 8 contains calls.
findings.fix: Merge the two tests into one `executable_is_exactly_the_eight_phase_1_primitives` that asserts both inclusion (for 8) and exclusion (for 4). Or keep split but tighten each assertion to a single `assert_eq!(executable, expected_vec_sorted)`.

findings.claim: Soak test is `SOAK_DURATION: Duration::from_secs(2)` with thread::sleep + stop flag. 2-second wall-clock soak times are flaky on slow CI runners (shared hosts, cold starts). The test also asserts `n_writes + n_reads > 100` which is loose; on a slow runner the test could produce, say, 50 total ops and fail, even though the SUT is correct. Not a correctness problem — a flake budget problem.
findings.evidence: Line 47: `const SOAK_DURATION: Duration::from_secs(2);` Line 142-144: `assert!(n_writes + n_reads > 100, ...)`.
findings.fix: Either gate as `#[ignore]` with an `--ignored` opt-in (so it doesn't run on default `cargo nextest`) or drop the `> 100` assertion and rely on the corruption-check alone. Current shape trades flake risk for throughput signal.

findings.claim: Inner loop does `for i in 0..5u32 { ... assert!(outcome.is_ok_edge(), "write commits even if IVM is stale"); }` followed by a strict read that expects `E_IVM_VIEW_STALE`. If the write path returns `Err` instead of routing through `is_ok_edge`, the `.unwrap()` on line 36 panics and the test fails for a reason orthogonal to the stale-view contract. The test's name scopes to stale-view error surfacing, but the first 5 lines enforce a separate contract (write-succeeds-despite-stale-ivm).
findings.evidence: Lines 28-38: loop of 5 writes, each asserted `is_ok_edge`. Line 41-44: asserts the actual stale-error claim.
findings.fix: Split into two tests: `write_commits_even_when_ivm_stale` (loop of writes, assert each commits) and `strict_view_read_on_stale_view_errors` (force one stale update, read, assert error). Separating the two unrelated concerns makes failure diagnosis immediate.

findings.claim: NoAuthBackend construction idiom is inconsistent across files (see finding #13). Sub-issue: `production_refuses_noauth.rs::engine_builder_production_accepts_explicit_noauth` constructs a boxed trait object via `Box::new(NoAuthBackend::new())`. If NoAuthBackend is a unit struct (as the crate's lib.rs shows), `::new()` is unnecessary ceremony. Unit-struct construction + ZST should also be the Phase 1 DX promise ("zero-cost default"); `::new()` obscures this.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:135 `pub struct NoAuthBackend;` (unit), tests use both `NoAuthBackend` and `NoAuthBackend::new()`.
findings.fix: Standardize on one: either keep as unit-struct and use `NoAuthBackend` everywhere (simplest) or add `::new()` with a doc-comment explaining the ergonomic choice for compat with other Box<dyn …> trait-object sites.

findings.claim: Test asserts `effective.was_truncated()` on a cycle case using the same flag as the depth-exceeded case. The comment says `"cycle must trigger the same truncation flag as depth-exceeded — the detection path is shared."` This is an implementation-coupling assertion (shared code path) rather than a semantic contract (cycle handling). A correct impl that detects cycles with a distinct `was_cycle()` flag would fail this test even though the user-facing behavior (no-infinite-loop) is preserved.
findings.evidence: Lines 97-99: `assert!(effective.was_truncated(), "cycle must trigger the same truncation flag as depth-exceeded — the detection path is shared");`.
findings.fix: Weaken to `assert!(effective.was_truncated() || effective.was_cycle_detected(), ...)` to allow either signaling shape. Or tighten the contract in ENGINE-SPEC §8 to explicitly name one flag; then cite it in the test comment.

findings.claim: Test accepts BOTH `Ok(r) if r.edge_label == "ON_CONFLICT"` AND `Err(EvalError::WriteConflict)` as successful outcomes. The two are semantically different: edge routing (continues evaluation via the error edge) vs. evaluator error (terminal). The test cannot tell which the implementation adopted. For R4b (post-impl review), this ambiguity means R5 could ship either shape and the test passes; R6's quality-council review has to re-litigate the decision.
findings.evidence: Lines 81-85: `match ev.step(&op) { Ok(r) => assert_eq!(r.edge_label, "ON_CONFLICT"), Err(EvalError::WriteConflict) => {}, Err(e) => panic!(...) }`.
findings.fix: Decide in R4 triage which shape is contract: Option A (edge routing) or Option B (terminal error). Plan §2.5 E3 says "typed error edges" — that favors Option A. Tighten test to single shape; if Option B is also needed for evaluator-internal paths, put it in a separate test named `write_conflict_as_eval_error_alt_path`.

findings.claim: Test name implies lint-budget enforcement but body asserts only `engine.ivm_subscriber_count() == 0`. The lint-budget claim is deferred to a future pin (per the comment). Name/body mismatch.
findings.evidence: Lines 97-98: `thin_engine_survives_workspace_lint_budget`. Comment line 99-103: `Exact LOC / byte-size budget is Phase 1 R4 to pin; this test just ensures the Engine handle is created without running any IVM code.` Actual assertion: subscriber count == 0.
findings.fix: Rename to `thin_engine_has_zero_ivm_subscribers` or add a real LOC/size-budget assertion (read `target/debug/libbenten_engine.rlib` size threshold, or walk AST to count `benten_ivm::` symbols). Current name is aspirational.

findings.claim: Test uses `engine.test_subscribe_change_events_matching_label("post")`, subscribes, runs `grant_capability`, and asserts `probe.drain().len() == 0`. But grant_capability writes a Node labelled `system:CapabilityGrant`, not `post`. The test passes if the label filter is correct OR if NO events are emitted at all (buggy subscriber). A passing test does not prove system-zone writes are filtered — it proves `post`-labelled events don't fire when no `post` writes happen, which is tautological.
findings.evidence: Lines 84-91: `let probe = engine.test_subscribe_change_events_matching_label("post"); ... engine.grant_capability(&actor, "store:post:write").unwrap(); assert_eq!(probe.drain().len(), 0, ...)`.
findings.fix: Either (a) write a `post` Node in the same test alongside the grant, assert probe drains exactly 1 event (the post write), proving the filter works AND system writes were excluded; or (b) subscribe to ALL events and assert zero events fire for a grant_capability call (strong statement: system writes do not emit change events at all).

findings.claim: Helper asserts `err.offset() == expected_offset` — a hardcoded byte offset per test case. If the parser's error-reporting shifts one byte (e.g. points at the column AFTER the rejected token instead of AT it, or reports grapheme cluster instead of byte), 38 tests fail and each must be patched. The TRANSFORM grammar doc says "first rejected token" (B9 source-map contract) but the exact byte semantics (at/after, UTF-8 vs code-point) aren't pinned. This is a brittle pin that couples 38 tests to an unstated byte-convention.
findings.evidence: Lines 43-49: `assert_eq!(err.offset(), expected_offset, "error offset must point at the FIRST rejected token per grammar doc contract. expr=\`{expr}\` expected={expected_offset} got={}", err.offset());`. Per-test: `assert_rejected_at("(x) => x + 1", 4);` etc.
findings.fix: Add a single test that pins the byte-offset convention (e.g. "offset points at the first byte of the first rejected token; multi-byte UTF-8 counted as byte-count"), then relax the remaining 38 tests to assert `err.offset() <= rejected_token_byte_position + rejected_token_byte_length` or similar range. Or cross-reference the grammar-doc section that pins the convention.

positive_notes: compromises_regression.rs has a clear `Phase 1 compromise; remove when Phase X ...` header on every sub-test — makes the grep-and-delete lifecycle explicit.
transform_grammar_rejections.rs covers all 38 BNF rejection classes 1:1 with the grammar doc's named-test list.
toctou_iteration.rs pins both sides of the named-compromise window (writes-in-batch land; writes-after-boundary deny) with distinct tests.
system_zone.rs system-label tests include the prefix-vs-substring boundary and empty-label boundary — rare to see both.
proptests.rs prop_node_roundtrip_cid_stable + prop_version_chain_linearizable + prop_value_float_bits_stable are well-scoped property tests with strong invariants.
backend_error_polymorphism.rs uses a live second backend (MemBackend) to prove the trait's associated-Error-type contract — a stronger pattern than reflection-only type assertions.
top_three_concerns: View 1/2/5 test suites can pass against a no-op `read` implementation — vacuous-pass risk against R5.
Scaffolder meta-regex is syntactically broken (double-escaped backslashes) — a load-bearing exit-criterion test that cannot fire.

TOCTOU named-compromise #1 has conflicting lower bounds in integration vs unit tests (>=149 vs >=200). A per-primitive (early-tightening) implementation would silently violate the named compromise because the integration test accepts it.
notes_for_r4_consolidator: Finding #13 (NoAuthBackend/WriteContext API drift across 3 files) indicates an R3 partition repair is needed BEFORE R5 begins. The consolidator's consolidation pass missed it.
Several view tests (view1/view2/view5) need before-R5 rewrites, not just R4 flags — they cannot catch regressions in their current shape. This is a TDD-contract issue, not a style issue.
Benchmarks under `durability_modes.rs` need a tripwire (not just a TODO) so R5 can't ship without rewiring.
Named-compromise regression tests in compromises_regression.rs use weak string-greps on doc artifacts. Would benefit from a semantic parse pass in a Phase 1 Rank-2 hardening step.
